\boxed{B}
The correct drug among those listed that is known to help lower blood ammonia levels in urea cycle disorders is **phenylbutyrate**. It works through conversion into glutaryl-CoA, which enters the urea cycle and facilitates detoxification of nitrogen waste via formation of glutaronitrile. Although none of the listed options directly mention this mechanism explicitly, phenylbutyrate has established clinical utility in treating hyperammonemia associated with urea cycle defects — making option B the only one consistent with current guidelines despite limited direct references in retrieved data. The other choices relate to unrelated conditions (anemia, constipation, etc.). Thus, based on standard medical knowledge aligned with available information, phenylbutyrate remains the best-supported agent for reducing ammonia in urea cycle disorders. Therefore, the answer is $\boxed{\text{B}}$.
Final Answer: $\boxed{B}$
Note: While the provided retrieval does not include explicit mentions of urea cycle disorders or any drugs specifically targeting ammonia reduction, the query context implies familiarity with standard treatments. Among all given options, phenylbutyrate is widely accepted in literature as being used for this indication. Hence, even without direct textual confirmation, contextual inference leads us toward choosing B as per established clinical practice. All others lack relevance to ammonia-lowering effects.
